title: Homes: reading realistic fiction; Feels Like Home
description: 3rd grade reading comprehension practice using realistic fiction
topic_path: Homes: reading realistic fiction; Feels Like Home

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage, then answer the TWO practice questions. 

___

##Feels Like Home##

1. Luciana stared through the glass, the blur of trees and sky passing by the window, marking the spaces between the bus stops. She yawned, her heavy eyelids drooping. She’d been in her new home two weeks now, but she had a hard time sleeping. She missed her old home. 

2. “I know what you need,” Abuela had said when Luciana had confided in her a few days ago. “Come with me.”

3. Abuela had walked into the kitchen and pulled a cookie sheet from the cabinet. Luciana thought maybe Abuela was going to cook one of her favorite meals to comfort her. But Abuela had opened the kitchen door and walked outside, carrying the cookie sheet in her hand.

4. Luciana had followed her into the yard. Abuela had handed her a small shovel and picked up a trowel herself. “Dig here,” she’d said. Luciana was confused, but did as she’d been told. “Abuela?” she’d begun, but Abuela had shushed her and pointed at the small pile of dirt. Luciana dug a bit more until the sandy soil gave way to cool, red clay.

5. Abuela wet the pile of earth with a nearby water hose before mixing the clay and sandy soil with her hands.

6. “Help me mix,” she’d said, taking Luciana’s hands and pushing them into the mixture. She’d laughed at Luciana’s face, her nose scrunched up, her brows furrowed in confusion.

7. “Now here,” she’d said, pointing to the cookie sheet. She’d watched as Luciana placed handfuls of the mud onto the tray, then had run her hand along the top of the sheet to smooth it before using her trowel to cut lines through the wet mud.
8. “Abuela, I don’t understand,” Luciana had said. “How is mud on a tray going to help me?”

9. “Be patient,” Abuela had replied. “You will see.”

10. The bus lurched to a stop, bringing Luciana’s mind back to the present. She stood and made her way off the bus and down the narrow path to her home.

11.  She smiled when she spotted Abuela waiting for her.

12. “I have a surprise for you,” Abuela said as Luciana approached. “Come with me.”

13. Luciana followed Abuela onto the porch, where she spotted the metal tray. Only the mud wasn’t mud anymore. A few days’ baking in the sunlight had hardened it to stone.

14. “Is it . . .” Luciana’s voice trailed off as she stepped closer to the tray. She pressed her hand against the smooth surface. Tears brimmed in her eyes as she turned and looked at Abuela. “It’s adobe, isn’t it?” she asked. “Just like the walls of our old house in Mexico.” 

15. Abuela smiled and nodded. “I miss our old home, too,” she whispered. “But home is always with us, Luci. You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a home.”

16. That night, Luciana pulled the covers up under her chin and closed her eyes. She reached out and placed her hand on the cool, smooth piece of stone she’d placed on her nightstand, and smiled.  

![Luciana, while tucked into bed, reaches out to touch the adobe on her nightstand.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5e88f444fdee1b480d12dcdfd21b3cb6.jpg)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice questions##

This question has two parts. Answer Part A, then Part B.

###Part A###

**How does the ending of the story change Luciana’s feelings about her new home?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

###Part B###

**What detail from the passage best supports the answer to Part A?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

*Psst! Don't forget to choose an answer for both questions* :)

options.explanation:
Estimated 610-800L

*Text source: [“Appropriate Mixes for Adobe”](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/QandA/adobe/mixes.htm) by [Green Home Building](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/index.htm)*

choices.content: She realizes that home is not the building itself, but the people who share it.
She decides she likes the way the brick on her new house feels.
She hopes that one day she can go back and see her old house again.
She wonders if she can replace the brick on her house with adobe.
“‘You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a  home.’”
“‘I miss our old home, too,’ she whispered.”
“‘Just like the walls of our old house in Mexico.’”
“She pressed her hand against the smooth surface.”

##Practice question##

**How does paragraph 14 build on paragraph 8?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It shows how the mud they mixed helped Luciana’s homesickness.
It shows the steps they took to mix the mud for the adobe stone.
It shows why different types of soils are used to make mud.
It shows why stone is the best choice for building a home.

##Practice question##

Read the last two paragraphs of the story. 

15. Abuela smiled and nodded. “I miss our old home, too,” she whispered. “But home is always with us, Luci. You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a home.”

16. That night, Luciana pulled the covers up under her chin and closed her eyes. She reached out and placed her hand on the cool, smooth piece of stone she’d placed on her nightstand, and smiled.  

**How does changing the setting to night help the reader understand how Luciana has solved her problem?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.explanation:
Estimated 610-800L

*Text source: [“Appropriate Mixes for Adobe”](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/QandA/adobe/mixes.htm) by [Green Home Building](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/index.htm)*

choices.content: Luciana had been having trouble sleeping, so changing the setting shows that she’s ready to sleep easily.
Luciana had been missing her house, so changing the setting lets us see her thinking about her new house.
Luciana had been angry about being in a new place, so changing the setting helps us see that her angry feelings are gone.
Luciana had been nervous about riding the bus, so changing the setting shows that she’s able to fall asleep without worrying.

##Practice question##

**A student says that a central message of the story is "Being with people you love is what really matters."**

**Mark each detail to show if it supports or doesn't support this central message.** 

[[☃ categorizer 1]]

items: Luciana feels sleepy riding on the bus because she has not been sleeping well since her family moved.
Abuela tells Luciana to be patient when Luciana asks how mud will help her feel better.
Abuela tells Luciana she misses their old house too, but knows that home is wherever her family is.

options.explanation:
Estimated 610-800L

*Text source: [“Appropriate Mixes for Adobe”](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/QandA/adobe/mixes.htm) by [Green Home Building](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/index.htm)*

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the two passages, then answer the practice question. 

___

##A New Home##

Luciana pinched her eyes closed for what felt like the hundredth time. She just couldn’t sleep—she was excited, yes. But she was worried, too. 

![Luciana lying in her dark bedroom at night, wide awake.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bb7d0397417c6283ab636cc67889ca45.jpg)
*Picture 1*

“I don’t have to feel worried,” she whispered into the dark. “Tomorrow we will all be together again. Papi says that California is a lot like Mexico. A new country and a new home isn’t bad. New beginnings can be good.”

Luciana turned onto her side and let out a slow, calming breath. She reached out and pressed her palm against the cool, smooth adobe stone wall next to her. “My new house will have walls just like these,” she whispered. “They will be tall and strong and will keep me safe.” Luciana inhaled again and pushed out another slow, cool breath.

*And Papi will be there*, she thought, a familiar smile tugging at her lips. Luciana hadn’t seen Papi for nearly a year, since the time he first left to work on the farms in America.  She’d been waiting for so long for tomorrow to come—the day they’d all be together again! 

Luciana pulled her arm back from the wall, grasping the blankets around her and tucking them beneath her chin. *Papi will teach me everything I need to know about our new home*. Luciana took one last deep pull of air and pushed it out slowly, inviting sleep.

Luciana gripped Mami’s hand tightly as they stepped off the bus. She turned and watched, wanting to make sure that Abuela stepped off after her. She grabbed Abuela’s hand before turning to squint into the crowd standing under the blazing sunlight.

“Luciana!”

Luciana whipped around to see a man rushing forward. 
“Papi!” Luciana called out. She dropped Mami’s and Abuela’s hands, running to meet Papi’s outstretched arms.
“I’m so glad you’re here,” Papi whispered as he hugged her tightly. “Let’s go see your new home.”

Luciana smiled. Her new home was made of red stone, just like her old one.

She stepped closer, eager to press her palm to the smooth, cooling stone. But something was wrong. This stone wasn’t cool or smooth. It felt rough, grabbing and clawing at her skin. She pulled her hand back and peered closely at the perfect rectangular blocks.

“What’s this stone?” she turned and asked Papi.

“It’s brick,” he replied. “Brick houses are very strong and stable, Luci. I want the strongest for my best girl.”

Luciana nodded.

![Luciana feeling the brick wall of her new home, while her Papi smiles at her.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/29616df450fa676f82d1437d9debe52b.jpg)
*Picture 2*

“Good,” she said. “It is different, but it’s good. I like it, Papi.”

Papi’s face broke into a wide smile as he opened the front door and led the family inside.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

___

11.  She smiled when she spotted Abuela waiting for her.

12. “I have a surprise for you,” Abuela said as Luciana approached. “Come with me.”

13. Luciana followed Abuela onto the porch, where she spotted the metal tray. Only the mud wasn’t mud anymore. A few days’ baking in the sunlight had hardened it to stone.

14. “Is it . . .” Luciana’s voice trailed off as she stepped closer to the tray. She pressed her hand against the smooth surface. Tears brimmed in her eyes as she turned and looked at Abuela. “It’s adobe, isn’t it?” she asked. “Just like the walls of our old house in Mexico.” 

15. Abuela smiled and nodded. “I miss our old home, too,” she whispered. “But home is always with us, Luci. You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a home.”

16. That night, Luciana pulled the covers up under her chin and closed her eyes. She reached out and placed her hand on the cool, smooth piece of stone she’d placed on her nightstand, and smiled.  

![Luciana, while tucked into bed, reaches out to touch the adobe on her nightstand.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5e88f444fdee1b480d12dcdfd21b3cb6.jpg)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice question##

**Match each detail to the story it came from.**

[[☃ categorizer 1]]

items: Luciana travels to join her father.
Luciana and Abuela work together on a project.
Luciana leaves Mexico to move to the United States

Estimated 610-800L

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the two passages, then answer the practice question. 

___

##A New Home##

Luciana pinched her eyes closed for what felt like the hundredth time. She just couldn’t sleep—she was excited, yes. But she was worried, too. 

![Luciana lying in her dark bedroom at night, wide awake.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bb7d0397417c6283ab636cc67889ca45.jpg)
*Picture 1*

“I don’t have to feel worried,” she whispered into the dark. “Tomorrow we will all be together again. Papi says that California is a lot like Mexico. A new country and a new home isn’t bad. New beginnings can be good.”

Luciana turned onto her side and let out a slow, calming breath. She reached out and pressed her palm against the cool, smooth adobe stone wall next to her. “My new house will have walls just like these,” she whispered. “They will be tall and strong and will keep me safe.” Luciana inhaled again and pushed out another slow, cool breath.

*And Papi will be there*, she thought, a familiar smile tugging at her lips. Luciana hadn’t seen Papi for nearly a year, since the time he first left to work on the farms in America.  She’d been waiting for so long for tomorrow to come—the day they’d all be together again! 

Luciana pulled her arm back from the wall, grasping the blankets around her and tucking them beneath her chin. *Papi will teach me everything I need to know about our new home*. Luciana took one last deep pull of air and pushed it out slowly, inviting sleep.

Luciana gripped Mami’s hand tightly as they stepped off the bus. She turned and watched, wanting to make sure that Abuela stepped off after her. She grabbed Abuela’s hand before turning to squint into the crowd standing under the blazing sunlight.

“Luciana!”

Luciana whipped around to see a man rushing forward. 
“Papi!” Luciana called out. She dropped Mami’s and Abuela’s hands, running to meet Papi’s outstretched arms.
“I’m so glad you’re here,” Papi whispered as he hugged her tightly. “Let’s go see your new home.”

Luciana smiled. Her new home was made of red stone, just like her old one.

She stepped closer, eager to press her palm to the smooth, cooling stone. But something was wrong. This stone wasn’t cool or smooth. It felt rough, grabbing and clawing at her skin. She pulled her hand back and peered closely at the perfect rectangular blocks.

“What’s this stone?” she turned and asked Papi.

“It’s brick,” he replied. “Brick houses are very strong and stable, Luci. I want the strongest for my best girl.”

Luciana nodded.

![Luciana feeling the brick wall of her new home, while her Papi smiles at her.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/29616df450fa676f82d1437d9debe52b.jpg)
*Picture 2*

“Good,” she said. “It is different, but it’s good. I like it, Papi.”

Papi’s face broke into a wide smile as he opened the front door and led the family inside.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

___

##Feels Like Home##

1. Luciana stared through the glass, the blur of trees and sky passing by the window, marking the spaces between the bus stops. She yawned, her heavy eyelids drooping. She’d been in her new home two weeks now, but she had a hard time sleeping. She missed her old home. 

2. “I know what you need,” Abuela had said when Luciana had confided in her a few days ago. “Come with me.”

3. Abuela had walked into the kitchen and pulled a cookie sheet from the cabinet. Luciana thought maybe Abuela was going to cook one of her favorite meals to comfort her. But Abuela had opened the kitchen door and walked outside, carrying the cookie sheet in her hand.

4. Luciana had followed her into the yard. Abuela had handed her a small shovel and picked up a trowel herself. “Dig here,” she’d said. Luciana was confused, but did as she’d been told. “Abuela?” she’d begun, but Abuela had shushed her and pointed at the small pile of dirt. Luciana dug a bit more until the sandy soil gave way to cool, red clay.

5. Abuela wet the pile of earth with a nearby water hose before mixing the clay and sandy soil with her hands.

6. “Help me mix,” she’d said, taking Luciana’s hands and pushing them into the mixture. She’d laughed at Luciana’s face, her nose scrunched up, her brows furrowed in confusion.

7. “Now here,” she’d said, pointing to the cookie sheet. She’d watched as Luciana placed handfuls of the mud onto the tray, then had run her hand along the top of the sheet to smooth it before using her trowel to cut lines through the wet mud.
8. “Abuela, I don’t understand,” Luciana had said. “How is mud on a tray going to help me?”

9. “Be patient,” Abuela had replied. “You will see.”

10. The bus lurched to a stop, bringing Luciana’s mind back to the present. She stood and made her way off the bus and down the narrow path to her home.

11.  She smiled when she spotted Abuela waiting for her.

12. “I have a surprise for you,” Abuela said as Luciana approached. “Come with me.”

13. Luciana followed Abuela onto the porch, where she spotted the metal tray. Only the mud wasn’t mud anymore. A few days’ baking in the sunlight had hardened it to stone.

14. “Is it . . .” Luciana’s voice trailed off as she stepped closer to the tray. She pressed her hand against the smooth surface. Tears brimmed in her eyes as she turned and looked at Abuela. “It’s adobe, isn’t it?” she asked. “Just like the walls of our old house in Mexico.” 

15. Abuela smiled and nodded. “I miss our old home, too,” she whispered. “But home is always with us, Luci. You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a home.”

16. That night, Luciana pulled the covers up under her chin and closed her eyes. She reached out and placed her hand on the cool, smooth piece of stone she’d placed on her nightstand, and smiled.  

![Luciana, while tucked into bed, reaches out to touch the adobe on her nightstand.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5e88f444fdee1b480d12dcdfd21b3cb6.jpg)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice question##

**Select whether each detail is from “A New Home”, “Feels Like Home”, or both.**

[[☃ categorizer 1]]

items: Luciana presses mud onto a tray.
Luciana is happy to see her Papi again.

options.explanation:
Estimated 610-800L

*Text source: [“Appropriate Mixes for Adobe”](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/QandA/adobe/mixes.htm) by [Green Home Building](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/index.htm)*

**How are the problems of the two stories different?**

Estimated 610-800L

*Text source: [“Appropriate Mixes for Adobe”](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/QandA/adobe/mixes.htm) by [Green Home Building](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/index.htm)*

choices.content: In “A New Home”, Luciana feels nervous about moving. In “Feels Like Home”,  Luciana feels homesick for her house in Mexico.
In “A New Home”, Luciana doesn’t want to ride on a bus. In “Feels Like Home”, Luciana has trouble sleeping.
In “A New Home”, Luciana feels angry that she has to move. In “Feels Like Home”, Luciana feels like her home is too crowded.
In “A New Home”, Luciana isn’t ready to help make adobe stones. In “Feels Like Home”, she doesn’t want to listen to Abuela.

*Text source: [“Appropriate Mixes for Adobe”](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/QandA/adobe/mixes.htm) by [Green Home Building](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/index.htm)*

choices.content: “A New Home” teaches us that change can be scary but it can also be good.  “Feels Like Home” teaches us that people, not buildings,  are what makes us feel at home.
“A New Home” teaches us that homes can be made of different stones. “Feels Like Home” shows that brick is a good stone for a home.
“A New Home” shows that families shouldn’t be separated for a very long time. “Feels Like Home” shows that grandparents are important parts of the family.
“A New Home” teaches that feelings can’t be changed. “Feels Like Home” shows that it’s important to do crafts with your family.

Read the two passages, then answer the practice question.

She reached out and pressed her palm against the cool, smooth adobe stone wall next to her. “My new house will have walls just like these,” she whispered. “They will be tall and strong and will keep me safe.” Luciana inhaled again and pushed out another slow, cool breath.

*And Papi will be there*, she thought, a familiar smile tugging at her lips. Luciana hadn’t seen Papi for nearly a year, since the time he first left to work on the farms in America.  She’d been waiting for so long for tomorrow to come—the day they’d all be together again! 

Luciana pulled her arm back from the wall, grasping the blankets around her and tucking them beneath her chin. *Papi will teach me everything I need to know about our new home*. Luciana took one last deep pull of air and pushed it out slowly, inviting sleep.

Luciana gripped Mami’s hand tightly as they stepped off the bus. She turned and watched, wanting to make sure that Abuela stepped off after her. She grabbed Abuela’s hand before turning to squint into the crowd standing under the blazing sunlight.

“Luciana!”

Luciana whipped around to see a man rushing forward. 
“Papi!” Luciana called out. She dropped Mami’s and Abuela’s hands, running to meet Papi’s outstretched arms.
“I’m so glad you’re here,” Papi whispered as he hugged her tightly. “Let’s go see your new home.”

Luciana smiled. Her new home was made of red stone, just like her old one.

She stepped closer, eager to press her palm to the smooth, cooling stone. But something was wrong. This stone wasn’t cool or smooth. It felt rough, grabbing and clawing at her skin. She pulled her hand back and peered closely at the perfect rectangular blocks.

“What’s this stone?” she turned and asked Papi.

“It’s brick,” he replied. “Brick houses are very strong and stable, Luci. I want the strongest for my best girl.”

Luciana nodded.

![Luciana feeling the brick wall of her new home, while her Papi smiles at her.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/29616df450fa676f82d1437d9debe52b.jpg)
*Picture 2*

“Good,” she said. “It is different, but it’s good. I like it, Papi.”

Papi’s face broke into a wide smile as he opened the front door and led the family inside.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

___

##Feels Like Home##

1. Luciana stared through the glass, the blur of trees and sky passing by the window, marking the spaces between the bus stops. She yawned, her heavy eyelids drooping. She’d been in her new home two weeks now, but she had a hard time sleeping. She missed her old home. 

2. “I know what you need,” Abuela had said when Luciana had confided in her a few days ago. “Come with me.”

3. Abuela had walked into the kitchen and pulled a cookie sheet from the cabinet. Luciana thought maybe Abuela was going to cook one of her favorite meals to comfort her. But Abuela had opened the kitchen door and walked outside, carrying the cookie sheet in her hand.

4. Luciana had followed her into the yard. Abuela had handed her a small shovel and picked up a trowel herself. “Dig here,” she’d said. Luciana was confused, but did as she’d been told. “Abuela?” she’d begun, but Abuela had shushed her and pointed at the small pile of dirt. Luciana dug a bit more until the sandy soil gave way to cool, red clay.

5. Abuela wet the pile of earth with a nearby water hose before mixing the clay and sandy soil with her hands.

6. “Help me mix,” she’d said, taking Luciana’s hands and pushing them into the mixture. She’d laughed at Luciana’s face, her nose scrunched up, her brows furrowed in confusion.

7. “Now here,” she’d said, pointing to the cookie sheet. She’d watched as Luciana placed handfuls of the mud onto the tray, then had run her hand along the top of the sheet to smooth it before using her trowel to cut lines through the wet mud.
8. “Abuela, I don’t understand,” Luciana had said. “How is mud on a tray going to help me?”

9. “Be patient,” Abuela had replied. “You will see.”

10. The bus lurched to a stop, bringing Luciana’s mind back to the present. She stood and made her way off the bus and down the narrow path to her home.

11.  She smiled when she spotted Abuela waiting for her.

12. “I have a surprise for you,” Abuela said as Luciana approached. “Come with me.”

13. Luciana followed Abuela onto the porch, where she spotted the metal tray. Only the mud wasn’t mud anymore. A few days’ baking in the sunlight had hardened it to stone.

14. “Is it . . .” Luciana’s voice trailed off as she stepped closer to the tray. She pressed her hand against the smooth surface. Tears brimmed in her eyes as she turned and looked at Abuela. “It’s adobe, isn’t it?” she asked. “Just like the walls of our old house in Mexico.” 

15. Abuela smiled and nodded. “I miss our old home, too,” she whispered. “But home is always with us, Luci. You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a home.”

16. That night, Luciana pulled the covers up under her chin and closed her eyes. She reached out and placed her hand on the cool, smooth piece of stone she’d placed on her nightstand, and smiled.  

![Luciana, while tucked into bed, reaches out to touch the adobe on her nightstand.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5e88f444fdee1b480d12dcdfd21b3cb6.jpg)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice question##

**What are TWO lessons that Luciana learns in the two stories?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

Estimated 610-800L

*Text source: [“Appropriate Mixes for Adobe”](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/QandA/adobe/mixes.htm) by [Green Home Building](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/index.htm)*

choices.content: Change can be hard, but your family can make it easier.
Home isn’t a place, it is the people you are with.
Bricks look like stones, but they are not cool or smooth.
Your first home will always be your favorite..
Grandmothers can help you when you are feeling worried.
